IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important and practical problem—stabilizing and improving end-to-end ASR by combining attention and CTC—explicitly proposing a joint CTC-attention model to mitigate attention alignment issues (Sections 2.3 and 3). The experiments on Japanese and Mandarin showing comparable performance to conventional hybrid systems without linguistic resources (Section 4 and abstract) make this approach likely to influence researcher and practitioner choices toward joint objectives. The method opens clear follow-ups (joint training/decoding, regularization of attention, monotonic alignment via CTC) and is readily adoptable in ASR toolchains, though its implications beyond sequence-to-sequence speech tasks are moderate rather than transformative.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized into standard sections (intro, method, experiments, appendices) and states the main contribution early (proposing a joint CTC-attention end-to-end ASR), but presentation problems reduce clarity. Notation and equations are mostly defined (e.g., p(X|S), CTC/attention equations) yet there are occasional inconsistencies and awkward notations (e.g., patt(C|X) placement, ‘Encoder(X) , BLSTM(X)’ formatting). The manuscript contains many duplicated/fragmented sentences, LaTeX artifacts and stray fragments (e.g., repeated sentences about end-to-end architectures, ‘LaTeX with hyperref package’, repeated citations), and references to figures/tables that are not shown, which disrupt reading. Overall readable and adequate, but significant copyediting and cleanup are needed to remove repetitions and fix formatting for smoother comprehension.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's mathematical formulations for hybrid HMM/DNN (Eqs.2–4,3) and attention (Eqs.8–11,21–23) are standard and appear correct; the motivation to combine CTC and attention is plausible and consistent with cited prior work. However, the provided text lacks complete derivations/algorithms for the joint training/decoding (no explicit joint loss weighting or decoding procedure shown here) and presents only fragmentary experimental reporting (datasets and baselines listed, but full quantitative results, comparisons and statistical significance are not included in the excerpt). Thus the core claim (joint CTC–attention yields comparable performance to hybrid systems) is plausible but not fully supported by the evidence shown. Minor technical details and comprehensive experimental evidence are missing in the provided content.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses end-to-end ASR combining CTC and attention (Section 3) — a central ML/NLP topic (speech recognition) — and explicitly targets problems (irregular attention alignments) of interest to this community. It provides standard problem formulation (Sections 2 and B), compares to hybrid HMM/DNN and CTC baselines, and evaluates on established benchmarks (Corpus of Spontaneous Japanese, HKUST Mandarin; Section 4), using expected training/decoding practices. Overall it is well positioned and uses methods/evaluations appropriate for a top NLP/ML venue.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper includes real experiments on two benchmarks (Corpus of Spontaneous Japanese and HKUST Mandarin) and compares the proposed joint CTC-attention system to hybrid and CTC baselines, gives some implementation/training details (encoder/attention type, input features, optimizer) and notes where it falls short of TDNN+LF-MMI. However, there are no clear ablation studies (e.g. varying CTC vs attention weighting, beam/coverage settings) or sensitivity analyses reported, limited discussion of failure modes beyond a few alignment examples, and reproduction details (exact model sizes, training schedules, hyperparameters, data splits, decoding parameters) are incomplete. Related work is reasonably well cited and limitations are acknowledged briefly, but the experimental analysis is adequate rather than comprehensive.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a joint CTC-attention network that combines benefits of CTC and attention during both training and decoding (Section 3 and description of two-pass rescoring), explicitly using CTC-based alignment to regularize attention and avoid irregular alignments (A, main text). It situates itself relative to prior work (notes Kim et al. 2016 used CTC as an auxiliary task) and claims a more rigorous constraint/guidance of attention by CTC, so the contribution is a clear, non-trivial extension rather than a purely incremental rehash. Given the combination of training-time regularization and decoding integration, the idea is notable and would be of interest to ASR experts, though it builds on well-known components rather than introducing an entirely new paradigm.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares its joint CTC-attention model against appropriate baselines (DNN-hybrid from the Kaldi recipe/Moriya et al. 2015, syllable-based CTC systems (Kanda et al.), and CTC+LM work (Miao et al.)), and evaluates on standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST). It cites and discusses the most relevant prior work (Chorowski et al., Graves et al., Kim et al., Chorowski & Jaitly, Povey et al.) and explicitly acknowledges when stronger systems (TDNN with LF‑MMI, Povey et al. 2016) outperform theirs and why. However, the provided text lacks full detail on exact parity of training/LM/decoding conditions (so fairness cannot be fully verified) and quantitative ablation depth is limited in the excerpt.
